questions.text: I feel that I was born with a naturally calm nervous system.
I feel that a raise or bonus is best used to help the people around me.
I rarely think about what someone left unsaid after a conversation.
I feel that my greatest accomplishment would be knowing every person in my life felt fully supported by me.
I hold the view that allowing suffering to shape my priorities is not a weakness but a sign that my values are active.
I pick up on the way someone chooses to introduce two people and what that framing reveals about their view of each.
I hold the view that my long-term wealth is one of the most important things I am building.
I am usually focused on the substance of a discussion rather than its emotional texture.
I notice the way certain people in a group look to one specific person before reacting.
I feel energized when the group collectively turns a situation around.
I am comfortable issuing ultimatums when I think they are needed.
I notice that excitement in me feels like a pleasant warmth rather than a roaring fire.
I feel that the pull toward action created by awareness of suffering is an expression of my deepest and most authentic self.
I often end up as the de facto leader in group projects.
My emotions inform me without overwhelming me — they are signals, not storms.
I believe that the moral pull of suffering is something I experience as a genuine force, not as an abstract principle.
My emotional states are so vivid that they feel almost cinematic.
I would cancel a personal trip to help a neighbor through an emergency.
I approach important decisions by gathering information and analyzing options systematically.
I believe that the capacity to think rationally about painful human situations without emotional contamination is what enables the most just outcomes.
I head out the door and figure out what I forgot along the way.
I notice that people look to me to set the standard of effort in a group.
I enjoy being part of a group that evaluates ideas together without veto power.
I tend to miss when a conversation has a dual purpose beyond its surface topic.
I give optimistic estimates and adjust timelines if complications actually arise.
I naturally defer to others in informal group settings.
I map out dependencies between tasks so I can work on them in the most efficient order.
I think that the ability to remain emotionally unaffected by the weight of someone's story is sometimes what allows you to help them most.
I sense when someone brings up a third party's name to indirectly communicate something to the person they are speaking with.
I experience relief as a full-body release, almost like physical collapse.
I tend to keep my most innovative ideas for my own ventures.
I routinely keep a list of personal achievements and review it for motivation.
I believe that approaching emotional situations with a problem-solving orientation is more genuinely helpful than approaching them with shared feeling.
I create project timelines with specific milestones and check-in points.
I find a certain creative energy in disorder that I lose when things are too tidy.
I find that my body often knows I'm upset before my mind does — tight muscles, shallow breathing, racing pulse.
